metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete Python type for the `jira` parameter of `classify()` (e.g., `tuple[ObservedJira, dict | None]` or a named `@dataclass JiraObservation(state: ObservedJira, fields: dict | None)`) so an agent can implement and test it without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The `classify()` function signature describes the `jira` parameter as 'ObservedJira-tagged snapshot value', which is not a concrete Python type — an executing agent cannot determine without clarification whether this is `tuple[ObservedJira, dict | None]`, a named dataclass, or a bare `dict` with an ObservedJira field stored inside it.
findings.location: New module: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/classify.py — `def classify(...)` signature
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small frozen dataclass: `@dataclass(frozen=True) class JiraObservation: state: ObservedJira; fields: dict | None` and use `jira: JiraObservation` in the signature. This makes the matrix cells readable (`jira.state == ObservedJira.PRESENT`) and is consistent with the plan's dataclass-first style.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the frozen-census assertion ('7 acting decisions' including PROBE_GET) with the circuit-breaker acting-kinds definition ({TERMINAL_TRANSITION, RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE, ADOPT}): either exclude PROBE_GET from the count and assert 6 acting decisions, or explicitly add PROBE_GET to acting kinds with justification.
findings.finding: The frozen-census test asserts 'EXACTLY 7 acting decisions' counting 5x TERMINAL_TRANSITION + 1x PROBE_GET + 1x ADOPT = 7, but the circuit-breaker section defines acting kinds as only {TERMINAL_TRANSITION, RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE, ADOPT} — PROBE_GET is NOT an acting kind. The test assertion and the acting-kinds definition contradict each other.
findings.location: Tests section (test_frozen_census.py) vs Circuit breaker section (acting kinds definition)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the frozen-census assertion to '6 acting decisions' (5x TERMINAL_TRANSITION + 1x ADOPT) and separately assert total_decisions==7 or decisions_by_kind[PROBE_GET]==1, keeping PROBE_GET outside the acting set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `CONFIRMED_404` observation value encodes 'grace met' before reaching `classify()` — either add a `grace_met: bool` parameter to the signature, or define a `CONFIRMED_404_GRACE_MET` sub-state in `ObservedJira`, so the matrix cell is unambiguous without duplicating the grace counter outside `BindingStore`.
findings.finding: The `RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE` decision cell in the matrix requires 'grace met' information, but the `classify()` signature takes only `ObservedJira`-tagged state — the `CONFIRMED_404` enum value carries no consecutive-404 count. The caller must pre-resolve grace-met status before calling classify(), but the plan does not specify this contract, leaving implementers to either: (a) always emit RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE on any CONFIRMED_404 (retiring after one miss — contradicts the existing grace-period design), or (b) duplicate the grace-count logic outside `BindingStore` (diverging from the existing `note_absent` authority).
findings.location: Section 'New module: classify.py' — matrix anchor cell: 'bound+*+CONFIRMED_404 (grace met) -> RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `ObservedJira.CONFIRMED_404_GRACE_MET` as a fifth enum value (the caller reads `absent_404_count >= grace` from the binding entry and tags accordingly), or add `grace_met: bool = False` to the `classify()` signature and document that callers read it from the binding entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm ADRs 0026, 0027, 0028, and 0029 are committed to `docs/adr/` before or alongside this ticket; the plan requires them as the primary specification but they are absent from the repo.
findings.finding: ADRs 0026, 0027, 0028, and 0029 are cited throughout the plan as the authoritative specification (matrix cells, baseline decision points, observation states) but do not exist in docs/adr/. Only ADRs 0001-0025 are present. The plan instructs implementors to 'read them FIRST', making the entire matrix spec unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: Plan Summary and every section that cites 'ADR 0026', 'ADR 0028', 'ADR 0029'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prerequisite task or an AC line: 'ADRs 0026-0029 are merged to the `docs/adr/` directory and linked from the plan before implementation begins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create (or confirm pre-existence of) ADR files 0026–0029 under `docs/adr/` before implementation begins, or update the plan to cite the actual location of the governing spec (ticket description, design doc, spike notes).
findings.finding: ADRs 0026–0029 referenced as the authoritative spec for the matrix, baseline decision points, and ObservedJira states do not exist in docs/adr/; the directory only goes through ADR 0025.
findings.location: Summary section: 'All invariants are specified by ADRs 0026-0029 (docs/adr/) — read them FIRST'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the four ADR files to docs/adr/ as part of this ticket's pre-work, or change the reference to point to the actual spike document (e.g. the 2026-07-03 spike notes referenced elsewhere in the plan).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `BreakerVerdict`'s complete value vocabulary (member names and type — e.g. `class BreakerVerdict(Enum): ALLOW | TRIP` or a frozen dataclass with `tripped: bool, acting_fraction: float`) in the circuit-breaker section of the plan.
findings.finding: `BreakerVerdict` is the declared return type of `check_blast_radius()` and a named field in the `census()` dict, but its value vocabulary (members, type — enum vs dataclass vs str) is never defined anywhere in the plan or the existing codebase.
findings.location: Circuit breaker section: `def check_blast_radius(...) -> BreakerVerdict`; also Census section: `{..., breaker_verdict}`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the circuit-breaker section: `@dataclass(frozen=True) class BreakerVerdict: tripped: bool; acting_fraction: float; acting_count: int` — or use `class BreakerVerdict(Enum): ALLOW = 'allow'; TRIP = 'trip'`. Either is fine, but it must be specified so the census record shape and the rollout-task consumer are unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the acting-kinds set: either add PROBE_GET to the circuit-breaker's acting kinds list, or remove it from the frozen-census acting count, so both sections are consistent.
findings.finding: The 'acting kinds' set used by `check_blast_radius` is defined as `{TERMINAL_TRANSITION, RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE, ADOPT}` in the circuit-breaker section, but the frozen-census test counts `PROBE_GET` as one of the 7 acting decisions (5x TERMINAL_TRANSITION + 1x PROBE_GET + 1x ADOPT = 7). These two specifications are contradictory: an executor implementing the breaker and the census test cannot satisfy both simultaneously.
findings.location: Circuit breaker section: 'acting kinds = {TERMINAL_TRANSITION, RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE, ADOPT}'; Tests section: 'asserting EXACTLY 7 acting decisions (5x TERMINAL_TRANSITION ... 1x PROBE_GET ... 1x ADOPT)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete Python type of `classify()`'s `jira` parameter — e.g. `jira_obs: ObservedJira, jira_snapshot: dict | None` as two separate arguments, or a `@dataclass(frozen=True) class JiraObservation: state: ObservedJira; fields: dict | None`.
findings.finding: The `classify()` function signature's `jira` parameter is specified only as 'ObservedJira-tagged snapshot value' — not a concrete Python type. An executor cannot write the function signature, docstring, or callers without knowing whether `jira` is a plain `ObservedJira` enum value, a `tuple[ObservedJira, dict]`, a tagged dataclass, or something else.
findings.location: New module section: `def classify(local: dict|None, jira: ObservedJira-tagged snapshot value, binding: dict|None, baseline: dict|None) -> Decision`
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two parameters: `jira_state: ObservedJira, jira_fields: dict | None` — this keeps the signature flat, matches the existing sentinel pattern in outbound_differ.py, and lets the Done-status matrix cell read `jira_fields.get('status', {}).get('name')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly document the source and sanitization procedure for `tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/` (what fields are stripped, what the snapshot skeleton contains) and confirm the directory will be created as part of this ticket's deliverables — it does not currently exist in the repo.
findings.finding: The frozen-census fixture path `tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/` (claimed to contain `bindings.json` + snapshot skeleton) does not exist in the repository. The plan states these fixtures should be 'checked in (sanitized)' as part of this ticket's deliverables. However, AC line 4 asserts 'frozen-census fixture checked in' as a pass criterion, and `test_frozen_census.py` depends on it — if the fixtures aren't present, the test file cannot be written and CI will fail.
findings.location: Tests section: 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/classify/test_frozen_census.py — replay sanitized frozen fixtures (tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/...)'; AC line 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deliverable to the plan: 'Create and commit `tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/bindings.json` and `snapshot_skeleton.json` (PII/body-stripped) as part of this ticket.'
findings.finding: The frozen-fixture directory `tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/` does not exist anywhere in the repo, and its parent `tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/` does not exist either. The plan requires this directory to be 'checked in (sanitized)' as part of this ticket's AC, but does not state it as a new artifact to be created.
findings.location: Tests section: 'frozen fixtures (tests/fixtures/bridge_convergence/frozen-2026-07-03/: bindings.json + snapshot skeleton, PII/body-stripped)'; AC: 'frozen-census fixture checked in (sanitized)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hypothesis>=6` to `pyproject.toml` `[dev]` dependencies (and pin or floor it consistently with ruff's pinning strategy) so `make test` can collect test_properties.py.
findings.finding: `hypothesis` is required by test_properties.py but is not declared in pyproject.toml's [dev] dependencies and has zero existing call-sites in the test tree, meaning `make test` will fail at collection.
findings.location: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/classify/test_properties.py (planned); pyproject.toml [dev]
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"hypothesis>=6"` to the `dev` list in pyproject.toml; optionally add a `[tool.hypothesis]` settings block to set a deterministic database path for CI reproducibility.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hypothesis` to `pyproject.toml` dev dependencies (e.g. `"hypothesis>=6"`) so `test_properties.py` can import and collect the four named property tests in CI.
findings.finding: `hypothesis` is not listed in pyproject.toml dev dependencies and has zero existing uses in the test tree, yet `test_properties.py` relies on it for four named property tests. This is an unverified capability assertion: the library may not be installed in CI, causing the entire properties test file to fail to import or be collected.
findings.location: Tests section / tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/classify/test_properties.py / AC line: 'hypothesis suite includes the four named properties'
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml under `[project.optional-dependencies]` → `dev`, add `"hypothesis>=6"` alongside the existing pytest/ruff/mypy entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `hypothesis` (e.g. `hypothesis>=6`) to the `dev` extra in `pyproject.toml` so `test_properties.py` is runnable under `make test`.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a `hypothesis`-based property test suite (test_properties.py with four named properties), but `hypothesis` is not declared as a dependency in pyproject.toml — it appears in neither `dev`, `agents`, nor any other extra. Running the property tests as specified will fail with ImportError.
findings.location: Tests section — tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/classify/test_properties.py; AC line: 'hypothesis suite includes the four named properties'
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml under `[project.optional-dependencies]` in the `dev` list, add `"hypothesis>=6"`. Also add `pytest-hypothesis` if targeted pytest plugin integration is needed, though `hypothesis` alone is sufficient for `@given` decorators.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan where (and by whom) raw HTTP/GET outcomes are converted into `ObservedJira` values — confirm whether `inbound_probe.classify_probe_response()` is the intended adapter or whether a new conversion is needed, to prevent an unintentional rebuild of existing classification logic.
findings.finding: The plan's new `ObservedJira` enum (PRESENT | CONFIRMED_404 | ABSENT_IN_WINDOW | TRANSPORT_ERROR) overlaps semantically with the already-shipped `ProbeBranch` enum in `inbound_probe.py` (PRESENT_RESOLVED | PRESENT_FILTERED | ARCHIVED_OR_MOVED | UNREACHABLE), but the plan does not specify whether `classify_probe_response()` will be used to produce `ObservedJira` values or whether a parallel HTTP→enum mapping will be written, risking partial NIH.
findings.location: New module: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/classify.py — ObservedJira enum; existing src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_probe.py — ProbeBranch + classify_probe_response()

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the explicit mapping from the existing `ProbeBranch` enum in `inbound_probe.py` to the new `ObservedJira` enum (e.g., `ARCHIVED_OR_MOVED` → `CONFIRMED_404`, `UNREACHABLE` → `TRANSPORT_ERROR`) so the boundary between the two classifiers is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The existing `inbound_probe.py` already defines a four-branch probe classification taxonomy (`ProbeBranch`: `PRESENT_RESOLVED`, `PRESENT_FILTERED`, `ARCHIVED_OR_MOVED`, `UNREACHABLE`) that partially overlaps with the plan's proposed `ObservedJira` enum (`PRESENT`, `CONFIRMED_404`, `ABSENT_IN_WINDOW`, `TRANSPORT_ERROR`). The plan does not acknowledge this existing classifier or specify the mapping/migration relationship between the two.
findings.location: New module section: `class ObservedJira(Enum): PRESENT | CONFIRMED_404 | ABSENT_IN_WINDOW | TRANSPORT_ERROR`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `classify()` accesses grace-met status for the CONFIRMED_404 cell — either document that `CONFIRMED_404` is emitted by the caller ONLY after grace is met (pre-filtering), or add a `grace_met: bool` parameter, or specify that `binding` dict contains `absent_404_count` and the threshold for classify() to evaluate directly.
findings.finding: The `RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE` matrix cell (`bound+*+CONFIRMED_404 (grace met) -> RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE`) requires the classifier to evaluate whether the grace period has been met, but the `classify()` signature provides no parameter carrying that information. The grace count lives in `BindingStore.absent_404_count` (binding_store.py:304), which is not accessible from the `binding: dict|None` parameter unless the plan specifies that the caller pre-serialises it into the binding dict — which it does not.
findings.location: Matrix anchor cells: 'bound+*+CONFIRMED_404 (grace met) -> RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE'; classify() signature section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (e.g., in `test_binding_baseline.py` or a new `test_config_schema.py`) that asserts `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` is present in the parsed config schema with type `float` and default `0.10`, making the AC #3 config-registration claim verifiable by `make test`.
findings.finding: AC #3 requires the config key `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` to be 'registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)' but no test or proving command is specified that would verify the registration is actually present and typed correctly — `make test` only proves this if a schema-validation test exists, which is not described in any of the five test files.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #3; Tests section (no test file covers _config_schema.py)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description in the Circuit breaker section of the _config_schema.py change required: the field path (e.g., reconciler.max_acting_fraction), its type (float, 0 < x ≤ 1.0), and any validation constraints, so the AC criterion has a corresponding plan-body description.
findings.finding: The AC requires 'config key reconciler.max_acting_fraction (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)' but the plan body never describes how or where _config_schema.py should be updated — there is no section on config schema changes, no description of the field type/validation, and no mention of _config_schema.py outside the AC.
findings.location: AC line 3 vs plan body (Circuit breaker section)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Circuit breaker section: 'Register reconciler.max_acting_fraction as a float field (0.0 < value ≤ 1.0, default 0.10) in the [reconciler] section of _config_schema.py using the existing typed-field registration pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the circuit-breaker section: the coercer type and validation bounds for `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (e.g. `_as_float(v, k, minimum=0.0, maximum=1.0)`) so the `_config_schema.py` entry is fully specified.
findings.finding: `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` is declared as a new config key to register in `_config_schema.py`, but the plan omits its coercer type, minimum, and maximum bounds — all required by the existing schema's per-key coercer pattern (float, minimum=0.0, maximum=1.0 is the convention for fractions in VerifyConfig, but is not stated).
findings.location: Circuit breaker section: 'config key reconciler.max_acting_fraction default 0.10 registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)'; AC: 'config key reconciler.max_acting_fraction (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan, add: 'Add `max_acting_fraction: float = 0.10` to `ReconcilerConfig` and `"max_acting_fraction": lambda v, k: _as_float(v, k, minimum=0.0, maximum=1.0)` to `_SECTIONS["reconciler"]`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the retire/cleanup path for the `baseline` field: either (a) confirm that retiring a binding removes the entire binding entry (including baseline) via the existing mechanism, or (b) add an explicit `clear_baseline(local_id)` call to the RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE handling path and document it here.
findings.finding: The retirement/cleanup path for the `baseline` field is unspecified: when a binding transitions to RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE or is otherwise removed, the plan does not state whether the baseline entry is deleted with the binding or accumulates indefinitely in bindings.json.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section: `baseline` field in BindingStore; Decision matrix: `RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test the re-run policy of `seed_baselines_from_snapshot`: either (a) skip keys that already have a `baseline` set (idempotent/resumable) or (b) document explicitly that it is destructive and must only be called once, with a guard that raises if any binding already carries a baseline.
findings.finding: The plan calls `seed_baselines_from_snapshot` a 'One-shot seed helper' but does not specify whether it is idempotent on re-run: if re-invoked on an already-seeded (or partially-seeded) store, it may silently overwrite existing baseline values that were deliberately advanced since the seed, or skip keys that failed in a prior partial run — the plan does not say which.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section — 'One-shot seed helper `seed_baselines_from_snapshot(prev_snapshot: dict)`'
findings.suggested_fix: Implement as skip-if-present: `if binding_entry.get('baseline') is None: set_baseline(local_id, fields)`. This makes the helper safely re-runnable after a partial failure and safe to call in CI retry scenarios. Add an assertion in `test_binding_baseline.py::test_seed_idempotent` that verifies a second call with a different snapshot does not overwrite existing baselines.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a proving command for the 800 LOC cap (e.g., `awk 'END{if(NR>800)exit 1}' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/classify.py` in a Makefile target or a wc-based CI step) or explicitly mark it as a reviewer-enforced guideline rather than a CI-enforced AC.
findings.finding: AC #5 states 'no file exceeds the 800 LOC soft cap' but provides no proving command or automated check for this constraint — `make lint` and `make test` do not typically enforce LOC limits.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief schema note (in an ADR update, CLAUDE.md, or inline docstring block that is explicitly treated as a contract) documenting the `decision_census` log record keys and their semantics so downstream consumers have a stable reference.
findings.finding: The decision_census telemetry record shape ({counts-by-kind, acting_count, acting_pct, breaker_verdict}) emitted via SyncLogger.log('decision_census', ...) is a new observable contract for any log consumer, dashboard, or alerting rule, but the plan specifies no schema document, ADR update, or CLAUDE.md note capturing this contract.
findings.location: Decision-census telemetry section; AC line 3 ('the record shape + unit test land HERE'); Acceptance Criteria line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to census() (or a short ADR 0029 amendment section) enumerating the record keys, value types, and what 'acting' means — this doubles as the contract reference and keeps the 800-LOC cap unaffected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a log/metric emission to `seed_baselines_from_snapshot` recording the count of baselines seeded, skipped (already present), and any errors encountered, consistent with the existing `SyncLogger` pattern.
findings.finding: The `seed_baselines_from_snapshot` bulk-write operation (613 keys) has no specified observability — no log, metric, or trace for success, failure, or partial-seed count.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section: `seed_baselines_from_snapshot(prev_snapshot: dict)`
findings.suggested_fix: After the loop, emit `SyncLogger.log('baseline_seed_complete', seeded=N, skipped=K, errors=E)` and raise (or return a Result) if `errors > 0`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the `RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE` env-var inconsistency in the plan (either add it to ReconcilerConfig in this ticket alongside max_acting_fraction, or explicitly document that migration is deferred to a follow-up).
findings.finding: Adding `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` to the typed config schema is correct, but the plan leaves `RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE` (the parallel reconciler safety knob in binding_store.py) as a bare env-var read via a local `_env_int` helper — an inconsistency the plan does not acknowledge. Since both knobs govern reconciler blast-radius safety, operators will face a split interface: one knob in rebar.toml, one in the environment only.
findings.location: AC line: 'config key reconciler.max_acting_fraction (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)'; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/binding_store.py:307 (_env_int('RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE', ...))

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the invocation site for `seed_baselines_from_snapshot` (e.g., a one-time CLI command `rebar seed-baselines --snapshot <path>`, a migration step in `BindingStore.load()` when `baseline_advanced_at` is absent, or an explicit ops runbook step) so an agent can implement the full integration without guessing.
findings.finding: The plan defines `seed_baselines_from_snapshot(prev_snapshot: dict)` as a 'one-shot seed helper' but does not specify WHERE it is invoked — whether it is a CLI command, a migration script run once, called during `BindingStore.__init__`, or triggered by a reconciler startup hook. An executing agent cannot implement the integration point.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section — 'One-shot seed helper seed_baselines_from_snapshot(prev_snapshot: dict)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-compat note for the bindings.json version bump: specify what old code does when it reads the new version (e.g., 'old code treats unknown version as an error and fails-closed' is acceptable IF that is the current behavior AND a rollback procedure for the file is documented; alternatively use a non-breaking additive-only change that does not increment the version).
findings.finding: The bindings.json version bump has no rollback/back-compat path for OLD code reading a NEW (version-bumped) file after a code rollback. The plan addresses forward compat (new code reading old files: 'absent baseline degrades to local-wins') but is silent on backward compat (old code encountering the bumped version field).
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section: 'bump the file version field; older entries without baseline are VALID'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE grace-period threshold is configurable (add a config key such as `reconciler.retire_grace_days` with a stated default to _config_schema.py), or explicitly document why it is intentionally fixed (e.g., 'defined in ADR 00XX as a fixed invariant').
findings.finding: The RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE decision cell depends on a 'grace period' threshold ('grace met'), but the plan does not specify whether this threshold is configurable, what its default is, or where it is registered — leaving it potentially hardcoded in classify.py with no config key.
findings.location: New module section: 'bound+*+CONFIRMED_404 (grace met) -> RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE'; Circuit breaker section (only max_acting_fraction is addressed there)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the single-writer contract for BindingStore (or add a file-level lock/atomic-rename guard) and assert it in a test or docstring so future concurrency changes don't silently violate the baseline integrity guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify concurrency ownership for `set_baseline` / `save()`: whether a single-writer invariant is enforced or assumed, leaving a TOCTOU race if multiple reconciler instances or threads share the same bindings.json.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section: `set_baseline(local_id, fields: dict)` + `save()`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to binding_store.py asserting 'single-writer per process; concurrent multi-process access is unsupported' and enforce it with a `fcntl.flock` advisory lock in `save()` if cross-process use is ever expected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly how `check_blast_radius` receives `max_acting_fraction` (e.g., as a plain parameter passed by the caller, not read internally), so the pure-leaf constraint and the typed-config registration requirement are both satisfiable without contradiction.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (default 0.10) is to be 'registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)', treating `ReconcilerConfig` as the landing site. The actual `ReconcilerConfig` in `_config_schema.py` has fields `jira_cli_timeout`, `lock_max_retries`, `deletion_probe_limit`, `id_guard_bypass_unsafe` — no `max_acting_fraction`. This is new work, but the AC states the field must be 'registered', implying it already has a home. More critically, `binding_store.py` uses env vars (not the typed config) for its own numeric knobs (e.g., `RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE`), and `classify.py` is specified as a pure leaf with no imports from the reconciler — so the plan doesn't specify how `classify.py` / `check_blast_radius` actually reads the config value.
findings.location: Circuit breaker section; AC line 3: 'config key `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the signature to `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction: float = 0.10) -> BreakerVerdict` (caller injects the value from config), and note that the caller (the future wiring task) reads it from `ReconcilerConfig.max_acting_fraction`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the AC to name the exact file path `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (ReconcilerConfig dataclass) and enumerate `src/rebar/config.py` as a consumer that re-exports ReconcilerConfig (no code change needed there, but it must be verified).
findings.finding: The plan names `_config_schema.py` as the file to receive the `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` config key, but does not specify it is `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — a file that lives outside the reconciler directory and is re-exported via `rebar.config`. The reconciler accesses config exclusively via `rebar.config.load_config().reconciler.*`, meaning the AC edit target is in a different package from the rest of this ticket.
findings.location: AC line: 'config key `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC to read: 'config key `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (default 0.10) added as a field on `ReconcilerConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`; no change to `src/rebar/config.py` re-exports required (field is accessible via existing `load_config().reconciler` path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `outbound_differ.BindingStoreProtocol` and `inbound_differ.BindingStoreProtocol` as consumers to assess: confirm that no call site passes a Protocol-typed store to `get_baseline`/`set_baseline`/`seed_baselines_from_snapshot`, or extend the relevant Protocol(s) if needed.
findings.finding: The two `BindingStoreProtocol` definitions in `outbound_differ.py` and `inbound_differ.py` are unenumerated consumers of `BindingStore`. While the new `get_baseline`/`set_baseline` methods are additive and will not break structural subtyping, `classify.py` receives a `binding: dict|None` parameter (not the store itself), so the plan's own interface design avoids this — but the plan text says `seed_baselines_from_snapshot` is a `BindingStore` method, meaning any code that passes a Protocol-typed store to seed will not type-check without Protocol updates.
findings.location: Per-binding baseline section: 'New methods `get_baseline(local_id) -> dict|None`, `set_baseline(local_id, fields: dict)`'; 'One-shot seed helper `seed_baselines_from_snapshot`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE` in the Decision matrix is intended to *replace* the existing `outbound_differ._note_absent()` call site (requiring a later wiring ticket to remove it) or to *model* it for census/breaker purposes only (in which case the live classify() consumer must not call `note_absent` again) — the plan must resolve this ownership ambiguity before implementers wire the consumer.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE` as a Decision kind whose consumer would trigger retirement, but the existing `outbound_differ._note_absent()` → `BindingStore.note_absent()` → `BindingStore._retire()` path already owns this state transition (with its own `save()` call inside `_retire()`). The plan is silent on whether the new Decision-based path replaces or coexists with the existing path. If both remain active (the plan says 'wires NOTHING into the live pass'), they will be double-executing: the existing outbound_differ will continue calling `_note_absent` and potentially `_retire`, while the classify()-based RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE Decision consumer (wired by a later ticket) would also trigger retirement — risking double-retirement side effects and making the 'acting_count' telemetry double-count retirements.
findings.location: Section 'New module: classify.py' — RETIRE_AFTER_GRACE Decision kind; Section 'Per-binding baseline' — 'no new commit path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and reconcile the config-consumption path for `reconciler.max_acting_fraction`: either follow the established reconciler pattern (`_env_int('RECONCILER_MAX_ACTING_FRACTION', 0.10)` in `classify.py`/`breaker.py`) and document that `_config_schema.py` is NOT the consumption site, or specify how the reconciler subprocess safely reads from the typed schema without breaking the stdlib-only isolation invariant.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` will be 'registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)', but the reconciler subprocess is an isolated stdlib-only process that never imports from `rebar._config_schema`. All existing reconciler numeric knobs use `_env_int()` / `os.environ` (see `binding_store._env_int`, `outbound_differ._env_int`) and `ReconcilerConfig` in `_config_schema.py` is only accessible to the in-process `rebar.*` library — not to the rebar_reconciler subprocess. The plan's config integration point is unverified against the actual consumption path.
findings.location: Circuit breaker section / AC line: 'config key `reconciler.max_acting_fraction` (default 0.10) registered in the typed config schema (_config_schema.py)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
